usage_note: Entity generation is driven by a deterministic takt time — a fixed or near-fixed arrival interval that represents the target production rate. Appliance assembly is typically paced rather than stochastic; variability enters through service time variation at stations, not through arrivals. This is the characteristic modeling feature of manufacturing lines that distinguishes them from service operations.
Appliance units differentiated by product family (refrigerator, washer, dryer, dishwasher, AC, range, oven) and SKU within the family, affecting which tasks are required at which stations.
Same as multi_class_entities at the customer-class level — different SKUs are different 'classes' on a mixed-model line with different service time profiles per station.
10-hour single-shift operation is the baseline; Scenario 4 explores multi-shift configurations. Start-of-shift warm-up and end-of-shift wind-down are modeling considerations.

Some activities (welding pipelines, high-voltage electrical work) require specially qualified workers. Inspectors are a distinct skill class from assembly workers. The PMC air conditioner paper's 41-worker crew split (34 assembly, 4 inspectors, 2 pre-assembly, 2 alternating) demonstrates skill-class specialization.
Stations are shared across multiple SKUs on mixed-model lines. Parallel stations share capacity between SKUs with different workloads.
Model-dependent task skipping — optional features on some SKUs are skipped at stations where they don't apply. Two-sided assembly lines for refrigerators have front/back coordination constraints.

Defects detected by quality inspectors route the affected appliance back to a rework station, creating a feedback loop. Typically modeled as a small fraction of the flow rather than a full separate loop.
Two-sided refrigerator assembly lines run front-side and back-side tasks in parallel with coordination constraints. Parallel stations at bottlenecks also create parallel paths that must re-merge.
